segments.text: Today I'm going to talk about retrieval and retrieval augmented generation.
So if we look at our standard prompting flow, normally what we do is we combine together
a prompt and play with an input. So if we say please answer this question, I think
Vin Diesel has been a voice actor for several characters in TV series. Do you know what
their names are? We could get a response from a language model, but there's several problems
with this. The first is athoracian issues. The models generally have a knowledge cutoff,
so the parameters are usually only updated to a particular time. So for example, if a new Vin,
Diesel TV series comes out, then the model that was trained up to a certain time point won't
be able to know anything about it. There's also issues in private data. So data stored in private
text or data repositories is not suitable for training for a number of reasons. Number one,
it's not available to particular language model training providers such as, you know,
OpenAI or Google or anybody else like this. The second thing is access control issues. So
even if you're within an organization that has lots of private data and you can train a language
model on that, certain people in the organization may have access to certain varieties of data
and other people may not. So it's not just solely an issue of third party providers, it's an issue
of organization level access control in general. In addition, there are learning failures. So even for data
that the model was trained on, it might not be sufficient to get the right answer. And this is
particularly the case for very, very large training data sets and models that are, you know,
modestly sized because the model is very often won't be able to learn from a single look at a
particular fact or whatever else like this, especially a bit of early training.
Another thing is even if the answer is correct, it might not be verifiable. So you might want to
be very sure that the model is not making any accuracy problems. And so in order to do that,
very often a human will want to go back to the source of the data.
So to solve this, there's a method called retrieval augment in generation, which will also be the
topic of our second assignment here. And the way it works is you retrieve relevant passages efficiently,
ones that can entail the answer to a question, and then read the passages to answer the query.
So we have documents like this. We have a query based on the query from retrieval. We get a
whole bunch of passages. We do reading, and then we get the answer.
So this is some fact implemented in many or even most language modeling providers,
including OpenAI. So to give an example, I asked the question that I just said about
from Diesel's voice acting and TV series. And Cheggy PT gave me an answer, and you can see that
Cheggy PT's answer includes several places with quotes. They're the little blue quotes there.
And if you click on the quote, it tells you where the information source came from. And so this
one says behind the voice actors, it's going to diesel. And behind the voice actors, TV shows big mouth
and diesel. Now, if we look closer into this answer, we'll see that it's not perfect even though it is
performing retrieval augment in generation. So, for example, I only asked about TV series, but it's
giving me lots of things about movies where it says Groot in Guardians of the Galaxy Volume 3,
2023 movie. And in fact, Vin Diesel was not even voicing a character named Groot here, so that's
definitely an accuracy mistake. And separately, there is a place where it says, additionally,
though the website for big mouthlets, Vin Diesel appears to be a misunderstanding or error,
as Nick Crowell is credited as the voice of Vin Diesel on that show. So there actually,
Nick Crowell was acting as Vin Diesel, but that's kind of a misunderstanding of the reader model.
But anyway, you can get the general idea here. You can also see that it's not perfect even for very
strong models like GPT-4. So now I'd like to go into the actual methodology that we use for this.
We have retrieval methods. And for the retrieval methods, we have quite a few different options.
I'm going to go through each one of them at a time. So Sparse Retrieval, Document Level Dense
Retrieval, Token Level Dense Retrieval, Cross Encoder Rearranking, and Black Box Retrieval.
So Black Box Retrieval, I'm not really going to go into it a whole lot. Basically, this is just
asking the Black Box Search Engine to retrieve the relevant context and getting the top
several results. Nonetheless, this is a pretty reasonable method to do it if you want to do search
over lots of data that exists on the internet already. And that, in fact, is what
a CHANJPT does. It looks up on the big by generating a query to the big.
So anyway, let's go into the actual methods that you develop and control yourself. So the first
one is Sparse Retrieval. And the way this works is you express the query and document is a Sparse
word frequency vector usually normalized by length. And so if I ask query what is an LP,
we get a vector where each row of the vector corresponds to a different token.
And we asked what is an LP. And so the places for what an LP and is will all have a non-zero value
and everything else will have a zero value. And we also normalized by the length of vector. So we
get something like 0.333.333. Then we have a whole bunch of documents. So the first document says
what is life. Candy is life. Someone really likes candy. We also have another one that says
NLP is an acronym for natural language processing. So this is a pretty good, you know,
answer to our question. Then we also have I like to do good research on an LP, which is, you know,
a nice sentiment, but not a very good answer to our question, I guess. So if we look at the
vectors here, we have what and candy and is have a fairly high score. And we have here NLP and is
have a high score and NLP has a non-zero score. So based on this, we find the document similarity
with the highest inner product or cosine similarity in the document collection. And so if we take
the inner product between these vectors, we actually see that the first one got the highest score
because of its relatively high values for the words what and is. So as you can see,
common words like what and is can get a high score kind of regardless of whether a document is
very relevant. And so one way we can fix this is through something called term waiting.
And the way that term waiting works is in addition to having this vector that calculates the
frequency within a particular document, we also have an up waiting term that gives higher weight
to low frequency words because low frequency words like NLP tend to be more informative about
whether the document is relevant than high frequency words like what it is because these high
frequency words like what and is could happen kind of regardless of whether the
you know, document is relevant the particular terms of person is asking about.
So one well used and easy to understand version of this is TFIDF or term frequency in document
frequency. So the way we define in term frequency is exactly what I talked about before. So it's
basically the frequency of the term T in the document D normalized by the total term frequency
within the document. So that's what I already showed in the previous slide.
And then in document frequency is a little bit more involved. But basically the way this works is
we have log of the total number of documents in the collection divided by the total number of
times this term appeared in any particular document. And so if a term appears many times in any
particular document it will have a low IDF score, one that's close to zero. But if it rarely appears
it will have a high IDF score. So basically this is up waiting our free term. And then for TFIDF
we basically multiply these two terms together and we up wait the
low frequency words. There's another version of this called DM25 that is
widely used.
